channel.title: Software Engineering on Engineering Notes
channel.description: Recent content in Software Engineering on Engineering Notes

item.title: The Doorman Fallacy and Why AI Keeps Failing at Replacing People
item.description:
In 2023, AI was supposed to replace programmers.

In 2024, AI was supposed to replace customer support.

In 2025, executives were talking about replacing entire departments.

By 2026, the pattern is more complicated. Companies are still automating aggressively, but many are also discovering that replacing a role is not the same as replacing the visible task that role performs.

That distinction sounds small.

It is not.

It is the difference between useful automation and expensive organizational self-harm.

item.title: AI Will Not Kill Junior Engineers but It Will Change Them
item.description:
There is a growing debate in software teams:

If AI can generate most entry-level coding work, do we still need junior engineers?

The question sounds reasonable. AI can write boilerplate, generate CRUD APIs, draft unit tests, explain unfamiliar code, produce documentation, create migrations, and fix simple bugs. A senior engineer with a strong AI coding assistant can now complete work that previously might have been handed to one or two junior developers.

item.title: Engineering Digital Twin - The Next Billion-Dollar AI Category
item.description:
Most AI tooling in software engineering is still obsessed with the same narrow promise:

What if developers could write code faster?

Useful? Yes.

Big enough to reshape how engineering organizations are run? I am not convinced.

The more interesting problem starts before anyone opens an editor. By the time a developer asks an AI agent to write code, a bunch of bigger decisions have already been made: what the business wants, which roadmap item matters, which architecture is acceptable, which team owns the work, what risk we are willing to carry, and what we are choosing not to fix.

item.title: Spec-Driven Development with spec-kit: Stop Vibe Coding, Start Specifying
item.description:
Most AI coding sessions start the same way. You open your editor, type a rough idea into the chat, and watch the agent generate files. It feels productive. Then two hours later you are reviewing code that solves a problem you never quite specified, built on assumptions the agent made silently, in a style that does not match the rest of your project.

This is vibe coding. It does not scale.

item.title: What If GitHub Was Built for AI Agents
item.description:
GitHub was built for humans reading diffs. But what happens when most of the code is written, reviewed, and merged by AI agents?

You do not just add an AI layer on top of Git. You rethink the entire system around how agents operate: intent, prediction, simulation, reputation, and policy.

Here is what an agent native source code management system could look like.

Intent Based Commits

Agents do not just commit code. They commit intent.
